text: The laparoscopic view reveals the uterus and bladder in the pelvis, surrounded by loops of bowel. The surgeon swings the laparoscope to the right, identifying the insertion of the round ligament near the pubic bone and a dimple indicating a potential indirect inguinal hernia. Upon further inspection, the area is identified as the cecum and appendix, with visible adhesions and scar tissue present.

The laparoscopic view shows the surgeon examining the area just to the side of the belly button along the abdomen, noting scar tissue suggestive of previous infection. The surgeon lifts the liver to expose the gallbladder, which appears normal, and inspects the upper abdominal area, including the diaphragm and liver, for signs of endometriosis. The stomach is visible on the left side, with the ribs framing the view.

The surgeon adjusts the operative bed to tilt the patient at a thirty-degree angle, elevating the hips and lowering the head to displace the small bowel from the pelvis for better visibility. The small bowel is visible, along with the large bowel and redundant sigmoid colon. Fluid is present in the pelvic area, obstructing the view, necessitating removal. The uterus is visible, along with the left and right ovaries.

The surgeon uses an irrigator aspirator to remove fluid, revealing invasive endometriosis on the right side of the rectum. Pigmented endometriosis is visible, and scar tissue is observed on the appendix, suggesting a need for removal due to suspected previous infection. The view shifts to the pelvis, showing the anterior cul-de-sac, Foley catheter, bladder, and uterus, all covered with clear vesicular endometriosis.

The surgeon uses a manipulator to tilt the uterus forward, revealing the posterior cul-de-sac for exploration. The ovary is visible, scarred to the side of the pelvis, with endometriosis present. The fallopian tube is also visible, along with various types of endometriosis, including clear, dark, and white areas, surrounding the uterus.

The surgeon focuses on the area near the right uterosacral ligament, identifying multiple clear and pigmented lesions of endometriosis that require removal. The redundant sigmoid colon is lifted to expose the posterior cul-de-sac, revealing invasive rectal endometriosis and numerous adhesions. The ovary is elevated to uncover additional endometriosis beneath it, characterized by fluffy, clear, and dark lesions.
